title: Generating the fermion mass hierarchy at the TeV scale
summary: We propose a class of theories to generate quark and lepton mass matrices where the scale of new physics is at the TeV scale, without inducing the large flavor and CP violating processes that are often thought to relegate the origin of flavor to energies above $\sim 100$ TeV. The models have new vector-like leptons and quarks, with mass mixings to each other and Yukawa couplings to light Standard Model fields encoded in "chains" reminiscent of dimensional deconstruction. Locality in the chains both generates the hierarchical Standard Model Yukawa matrices, and ensures that CP and flavor violating effects are small, even with the vector-like particles at the TeV scale. A simple extension also generates neutrino masses, whose tiny size is parametrically related to the square of the electron Yukawa coupling. We outline the essential features of these models, explain how fermion mass hierarchies and mixing angles emerge, and explore their phenomenological implications. This framework can be tested both in the final run of the LHC as well as at possible future colliders operating at the 10 TeV scale, and we identify some of the distinctive experimental signatures associated with the production and decay of the new vector-like fermions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a theoretical framework with TeV-scale vector-like fermions to address quark and lepton mass hierarchies and flavor/CP violation, avoiding suppression to higher energy scales. It uses 'deconstructed chains' for Yukawa structures and natural suppression of unwanted processes.
AI.motivation: To solve the flavor hierarchy problem and suppress large flavor/CP violating processes, traditionally requiring new physics above ~100 TeV, by proposing a model where new physics is at the TeV scale.
AI.method: Introduce vector-like leptons and quarks with mass mixings and Yukawa couplings organized in 'chains' (dimensional deconstruction). This arrangement generates hierarchical Standard Model Yukawas and protects against excessive flavor/CP violation.
AI.result: Framework naturally explains fermion mass hierarchies and mixing angles, avoids excessive flavor violation at TeV scales, and provides testable predictions for LHC and future colliders. Neutrino masses emerge as a byproduct proportional to the square of the electron Yukawa coupling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed models offer a viable alternative to high-scale physics solutions, presenting a falsifiable mechanism testable at upcoming colliders through vector-like fermion signatures.

title: Minimal Dark Matter: Generalized Framework and Direct-Detection Sensitivity
summary: Minimal electroweak dark matter models are compelling due to their simplicity, though calculations of their freezeout abundance are complicated by nonperturbative effects due to Sommerfeld enhancement and bound-state formation. It has been shown that all individual multiplet scenarios beyond the doublet lead to direct-detection signals above the neutrino floor and thus within the reach of next-generation experiments. If no signals are found, would minimal dark matter be excluded? Yes for the simplest models, but it has been unknown for the important extension of two multiplets coupled by Higgs interactions (Higgs-coupled minimal dark matter). We present a generalized framework for calculating nonperturbative effects for such models that also covers the case of individual multiplets. In this framework, we calculate nonperturbative effects on freezeout as well as the prospects for direct detection, correcting shortcomings and omissions in the literature. Importantly, for the mixed Majorana (odd) and Dirac (even) multiplet combinations 3M2D, 5M4D, and 7M6D, we find that the predicted direct-detection signals can extend below the neutrino floor. Fully testing minimal dark matter will thus require more than direct-detection experiments.

AI.tldr: Minimal electroweak dark matter models with two multiplets coupled by Higgs interactions can produce direct-detection signals below the neutrino floor, meaning some models may avoid exclusion even if next-gen experiments find no signals. A generalized framework addressing nonperturbative effects like Sommerfeld enhancement and bound-state formation is developed for these models.
AI.motivation: To determine whether minimal dark matter models, particularly those with two multiplets interacting via Higgs couplings, could be excluded if future experiments detect no signals, addressing gaps in existing literature.
AI.method: Developed a framework to calculate nonperturbative effects (Sommerfeld enhancement, bound states) on freezeout and direct detection for both single and two-multiplet scenarios, correcting previous omissions.
AI.result: Found that mixed Majorana/Dirac multiplet combinations (3M2D, 5M4D, 7M6D) can have direct detection signals below the neutrino floor, meaning direct detection alone won't fully test minimal dark matter.
AI.conclusion: Minimal dark matter models with Higgs-coupled two multiplets aren't fully testable by direct detection experiments alone; additional methods are needed to validate or exclude these models.

title: Next-to-Leading Order QCD Corrections to $Λ_b \to p $ Form Factors from Light-Cone Sum Rules
summary: In this study, we compute the radiative corrections to the $Λ_b \to p$ transition form factors at next-to-leading logarithmic accuracy, employing the framework of QCD light-cone sum rules with the light-cone distribution amplitudes of the $Λ_b$ baryon. The factorization formulae of the vacuum-to-$Λ_b$ correlation function, constructed from the interpolating current for the proton, are derived at leading power in $m_p / m_{Λ_b}$, using the method of regions. With our specific choice of interpolating current, only the twist-4 distribution amplitude of the $Λ_b$ baryon contributes to the form factors. Numerically, we find that the next-to-leading order QCD perturbative corrections reduce the tree-level form factors to approximately 65$\%$ of their original value, with the next-to-leading-order jet function providing the dominant contribution. In the large-energy limit ($E_p \to \infty$), the form factors exhibit a clear $1/E_p^3$ scaling, consistent with the expected power-counting behavior. By applying the $z$-series parameterization to perform a combined fit of the form factors from our results and available lattice QCD simulations, we further investigate the decay rate of $Λ_b \to p \ell^- \barν_{\ell}$ and extract the CKM matrix element $|V_{ub}| = (3.33\pm 0.43 ) \times 10^{-3}$.

AI.tldr: The study calculates radiative corrections to $Λ_b → p$ transition form factors using QCD light-cone sum rules, finding a 35% reduction from next-to-leading order effects and determining $|V_{ub}| = (3.33±0.43) × 10^{-3}$ through combined lattice/fit analysis.
AI.motivation: To improve precision in $Λ_b → p$ form factor predictions and extract CKM matrix element $V_{ub}$ by including higher-order QCD corrections and cross-validating with lattice data.
AI.method: Uses QCD light-cone sum rules with $Λ_b$ baryon distribution amplitudes, derivation of vacuum-to-$Λ_b$ correlation functions via method of regions, and $z$-series parameterization for form factor fitting incorporating lattice QCD results.
AI.result: Next-to-leading order corrections reduce form factors to ~65% of tree-level values, form factors follow $1/E_p^3$ scaling at high energy, and extracted $|V_{ub}| = (3.33±0.43) × 10^{-3}$.
AI.conclusion: The calculations confirm expected QCD power-counting behavior and provide a precise $V_{ub}$ determination, highlighting the necessity of including radiative corrections and lattice data for accurate heavy quark transition analyses.

title: Dark Photon mediated Inelastic Dark Matter in Cosmology, Astrophysics and Colliders
summary: We provide a systematic discussion of the phenomenology of Dark Photon iDM (A$^{\prime}$iDM) where the Standard Model (SM) is extended by a dark sector containing an additional $U(1)_D$ gauge symmetry under which all SM particles are neutral, and that couples to the SM hypercharge gauge boson through a kinetic mixing parameter $ε$. The model contains two Majorana states $χ_1$ and $χ_2$ with $δ=M_{χ_2}-M_{χ_1}>0$ and $χ_1$ the dark matter candidate, and a dark photon $A^{\prime}$ with mass $M_{A^{\prime}}$. Our analysis represents an integration of existing ones, where only specific benchmarks of the A$^{\prime}$iDM scenario have been discussed. In particular, we fix the $U(1)_D$ coupling $α_D$ equal to the electromagnetic one $α_{EM}$ and $ε$ to its experimental upper bound, and perform a complete scan of the remaining parameters $(M_{χ_1},δ, M_{A^{\prime}})$, discussing the $χ_1$ relic abundance, its direct and indirect searches, as well as potential signals from astrophysics and accelerators. Our systematic scan shows that $α_D$ = $α_{EM}$ is not disfavored, as some previous analyses, limited to specific benchmarks, may suggest. We also find that when the $χ_1$ relic density matches observation direct and indirect searches are not kinematically accessible. On the other hand we find that the projected luminosity of FASER, a detector searching for Long Lived Particles (LLP) decay at the LHC, can probe or rule out the parameters space of the model for $M_{χ_1}\lesssim$ 7 GeV, 100 MeV $\lesssim δ\lesssim$ 300 MeV and $M_{A^{\prime}}\lesssim$ 25 GeV. This range of parameter could be significantly extended by the FASER 2 upgrade proposed for the High-Luminosity phase at the LHC. The parameter space probed by LLP seaches partially overlaps with that probed by $χ_1$ capture in neutron stars.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically analyzes the Dark Photon iDM (A' iDM) model, exploring its phenomenology through a comprehensive parameter scan. It finds that the model remains viable with α_D = α_EM and is not excluded by current data, with FASER at the LHC poised to probe key parameter regions, especially with future upgrades.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a comprehensive analysis of the A' iDM model beyond previous limited benchmarks, addressing the viability of the model when α_D equals electromagnetism's coupling constant and ε is at its experimental upper bound. This work aims to fill gaps in understanding the model's parameter space and its testability through astrophysical and accelerator experiments.
AI.method: The authors fix α_D = α_EM and ε to its upper limit, then perform a systematic scan over the remaining parameters (M_{χ_1}, δ, M_{A'}). They compute the dark matter relic abundance, assess compatibility with direct/indirect detection experiments, and evaluate potential signals from astrophysics and LHC-based detectors like FASER.
AI.result: The analysis shows α_D = α_EM isn't disfavored, contrary to some previous studies. For parameter regions matching observed relic density, direct/indirect searches are kinematically inaccessible. FASER's projected luminosity can probe M_{χ_1} ≤7 GeV, 100-300 MeV δ, and M_{A'} ≤25 GeV. FASER2 upgrades and neutron star captures show overlapping probing potential.
AI.conclusion: The model remains viable under the stated conditions, with FASER and its successors critical in testing the parameter space, particularly in regions inaccessible to current dark matter detection methods. Future high-luminosity LHC upgrades enhance the model's testability, emphasizing the importance of LLP searches.

title: There and back again -- Closed timelike curves as EFT selection principle
summary: Modified gravity is often approached in the context of effective-field theory (EFT), with the view that the EFT corrections permit a more desirable theory. In this paper, we posit that this should extend to the causal structure of curved spacetime in addition to the standard demands such that of flat spacetime positivity and unitarity. We propose a new guiding principle for modified-gravity theories, namely that closed timelike curves should always be {\it harder} to obtain than in General Relativity. By demanding this, one can place powerful constraints on modified gravity. To elucidate this claim, we investigate modified-gravity EFTs on rotating black-hole backgrounds, focusing on the appearance/disappearance of closed timelike curves, and provide parameter bounds which only partly overlap with other approaches based on time delay. We construct perturbative rotating black-hole solutions in modified-gravity EFTs based on the Horndeski class and provide parameter bounds necessary to preserve causality and stability. Finally, we present a novel probe for the existence of closed timelike curves through quasinormal modes and black-hole echoes. This can be used to diagnose spacetime causality once next-generation gravitational-wave data becomes available.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new principle for modified gravity theories to make closed timelike curves (CTCs) harder to form than in General Relativity, using effective-field theory on rotating black holes to derive parameter constraints and introduces quasinormal modes as a probe for CTCs.
AI.motivation: To address the issue that existing modified gravity approaches focus on effective field theory corrections but neglect causal structure considerations. Ensuring causality by preventing CTCs more effectively than GR is crucial for viable theories.
AI.method: 1. Propose a principle that CTCs should be harder to obtain in modified gravity than in GR. 2. Study modified-gravity EFTs on rotating black holes to analyze CTC appearance/disappearance. 3. Derive parameter bounds for Horndeski class theories. 4. Introduce quasinormal mode analysis for detecting CTCs via gravitational waves.
AI.result: Parameter constraints in Horndeski EFT that overlap partially with time delay methods. Novel diagnostic tool (quasinormal modes/black-hole echoes) for detecting CTCs, applicable once next-gen GW data is available.
AI.conclusion: Causal structure constraints complement existing criteria in modified gravity. Future gravitational wave observations could test these theories using the proposed probes, ensuring causality and stability in extended gravity frameworks.

title: Undetected past contacts with technological species: implications for technosignature science
summary: In the search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI), the highly incomplete sampling of the technosignature search space is often considered as a plausible explanation for the persistent lack of detections over six decades of searches. If correct, this would imply that technosignatures may already have reached Earth without being detected or correctly identified. Here, we explore this possibility using a Bayesian inference framework to estimate present-day detectability given $n\ge 1$ undetected contacts over the past 65 years -- the period since the first SETI experiment. We show that achieving high detectability of technosignatures emitted within a few hundred light-years of Earth would require implausibly large $n$ values, even exceeding the population of habitable planets within that range. More conservative estimates can be obtained only assuming that emitters are tightly clustered near Earth or that their population in the Milky Way has undergone a very recent and sudden boost. This tension is further exacerbated for short-lived technosignatures and persists whether they are omnidirectional, as in Dysonian megastructures, or directional, as in intentional communication attempts. These findings suggest that, if undetected past contacts from the Milky Way have indeed occurred, the best prospects of detection may lie in searches extending over several thousand light-years, though only a few detectable technoemissions would be expected.

AI.tldr: The paper uses Bayesian inference to analyze the likelihood of undetected extraterrestrial technosignatures, finding that high detectability within a few hundred light-years requires implausibly many undetected signals, suggesting future searches need to target greater distances.
AI.motivation: To explain the lack of SETI detections over 65 years by assessing the role of incomplete sampling. The authors explore whether undetected technosignatures might already exist but have gone unnoticed, requiring large numbers of missed signals to justify current detectability limits.
AI.method: Bayesian framework estimating detectability based on the number of undetected contacts (n >=1) over 65 years. Analyzes scenarios where detectable signals exist within a few hundred light-years, considering both omnidirectional (Dyson spheres) and directional (communications) signals.
AI.result: Achieving high detectability requires implausibly high n values exceeding local habitable planets unless signals cluster near Earth or emitter populations surged recently. Short-lived signals worsen this tension. Conservative detectability parameter values necessitate extending searches to thousands of light-years.
AI.conclusion: Undetected extraterrestrial technosignatures are unlikely within a few hundred light-years under plausible assumptions. Future SETI efforts must prioritize vast search volumes and accept low-detection expectations, challenging the 'incomplete sampling' explanation unless specific emitter distribution assumptions hold.

title: Relativistic Magnetohydrodynamic Simulations of Giant Magnetar Bursts
summary: Gradual crustal deformation can generate strongly twisted magnetic fields around magnetars, potentially triggering giant flares with total energies exceeding $10^{44}\,\mathrm{erg}$. In this Letter, we present the first relativistic magnetohydrodynamic simulation of a surface shear-driven magnetar eruption, capturing reconnection-driven plasma heating, the ejection of relativistically hot plasma, and the formation of a hot fireball confined within the inner magnetosphere. We find that magnetic reconnection in the equatorial current sheet launches a hot trailing outflow capable of powering the initial spike observed in giant flares, while simultaneously leaving behind a thermally stratified fireball with sufficient thermal energy to produce the pulsating, decaying tail. Together, these features provide a self-consistent physical framework for understanding the observed energetics of magnetar giant flares. The eruption also expels a magnetically dominated giant plasmoid carrying up to $\sim 9\%$ of the magnetosphere's total magnetic energy. Furthermore, our simulation demonstrates how the plasmoid drives the formation of a blast wave -- an important ingredient in models linking magnetar eruptions to fast radio bursts.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the first relativistic magnetohydrodynamic simulation of a magnetar eruption driven by surface shear, explaining key features of giant flares through magnetic reconnection and plasmoid dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how gradual crustal deformation in magnetars triggers giant flares with energies exceeding 10^44 erg, and to model the observed characteristics of these flares, including initial spikes and decaying tails.
AI.method: Relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations were conducted to model surface shear-driven eruptions, focusing on reconnection-driven plasma heating, relativistic plasma ejection, and fireball formation within the magnetosphere.
AI.result: Simulation revealed that magnetic reconnection in the equatorial current sheet generates a trailing outflow powering the initial flare spike and a thermally stratified fireball explaining the pulsating tail. A magnetically dominated plasmoid carrying ~9% of the magnetosphere's energy was ejected, driving a blast wave relevant to fast radio burst models.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a unified framework linking crustal deformation, magnetic reconnection, and plasmoid dynamics to the energetics and observational features of magnetar giant flares and potential fast radio burst precursors.

title: Signs of universality in the behavior of elastic $\textit{pp}$ scattering cross-sections at high energies
summary: We give a phenomenological analysis of the behavior of inelastic, elastic and total cross-sections of the high energy $\textit{pp}$ interaction. In particular, we argue that the universal picture of behavior of cross-sections and their ratios is a consequence of the rapid increase of inelastic cross-section with energy and its large value compared to $σ_{\mathrm{el} }(s)$. We observed that the value of the fundamental ratio $(m_{π^{\mathrm{0}}}/m_{p}) $, the minimum value of the ratio $ (σ_{\mathrm{el}}/σ_{\mathrm{tot}})$, and some other quantities are determined by the roots of the equation $ (9\,x^{2}+4\,\sqrt{2}\,x-1)=0 $.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes high-energy pp interactions, showing that the behavior of cross-sections and their ratios stems from the inelastic cross-section's rapid growth and dominance over elastic cross-sections. Key quantities are linked to the roots of a specific quadratic equation.
AI.motivation: To understand the universal behavior of cross-sections in high-energy pp collisions by examining the relationship between inelastic and elastic cross-sections and identifying fundamental ratios determined by a mathematical equation.
AI.method: Phenomenological analysis of inelastic, elastic, and total cross-sections' behavior, focusing on their ratios and the role of the inelastic cross-section's energy dependence. The roots of the equation 9x² + 4√2 x -1 = 0 are used to determine key quantities like (m_π⁰/m_p) and the minimum (σ_el/σ_tot).
AI.result: The analysis reveals that the universal cross-section behavior arises from the inelastic cross-section's dominance. The equation's roots explain fundamental ratios, connecting particle mass ratios and cross-section minima.
AI.conclusion: The observed ratios and behaviors in high-energy pp interactions are fundamentally tied to the mathematical solutions of the given equation, highlighting a universal structure in particle collision dynamics.

title: Scaling Laws for Template-Free Detection of Environmental Phase Modulation in Gravitational-Wave Signals
summary: Environmental effects such as hierarchical triple motion can introduce cumulative phase modulation in gravitational-wave signals through time-dependent line-of-sight acceleration. Whether such smooth time-warp distortions are observable depends jointly on deformation strength and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), yet this relationship has not been quantified in a template-free framework. We study the detectability of these distortions using time-frequency representations derived from the continuous wavelet transform. Instead of reconstruction error alone, we examine trajectory-based statistics, in particular the evolution of the power-weighted frequency centroid. We find that environmental modulation can be detected using a single-sample statistic referenced to an isolated-binary distribution, without requiring matched templates. Across a grid of cumulative phase distortions and SNR, detection performance collapses onto a single scaling parameter defined as the product of phase distortion and SNR. The ROC-AUC follows a sigmoid transition in this parameter. Moderate distortions are detectable at low SNR, whereas smaller distortions require higher SNR. These results indicate that smooth environmental phase modulation is not generically absorbed by intrinsic waveform variability; instead, detectability is governed by a simple scaling between cumulative phase distortion and signal strength.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that time-frequency analysis can detect environmental phase modulation in gravitational-wave signals based on a scaling relation between phase distortion and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), enabling detection without matched templates.
AI.motivation: To determine if smooth time-warp distortions from environmental effects are observable, and quantify their detectability in a template-free framework, addressing the lack of prior analysis on the relationship between deformation strength and SNR.
AI.method: Uses time-frequency representations (continuous wavelet transform) and trajectory-based statistics (power-weighted frequency centroid evolution) to analyze signal distortions without relying on waveform templates.
AI.result: Detection performance collapses onto a scaling parameter (phase distortion × SNR), with ROC-AUC showing a sigmoid transition. Moderate distortions detectable at low SNR; smaller distortions require higher SNR.
AI.conclusion: Environmental phase modulation isn’t absorbed by intrinsic waveform variations; detectability hinges on a simple scaling between cumulative phase distortion and signal strength.

title: Improved Identification of Satellite Trails in ACS/WFC Imaging Using a Modified Radon Transform
comment: 25 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables. Code available at https://acstools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/findsat_mrt.html
summary: We present a new approach to identify satellite trails (or other linear artifacts) in ACS/WFC imaging data using a modified Radon Transform. We demonstrate that this approach is sensitive to features with mean brightness significantly below the background noise level, and it is resistant to the influence of bright astronomical sources (e.g., stars, galaxies) in most cases. Comparing with a set of satellite trails identified by eye, we find a trail recovery rate of 85\% and a false detection rate (after removing diffraction spikes that are easily filtered) of 2.5\%. By performing an analysis using a much larger ACS/WFC data set where false trails are identified by their persistence across multiple images of the same field, we identify the Radon Transform parameter space and image properties where our algorithm is unreliable, and estimate a false detection rate of $\sim10\%$ elsewhere. We apply our method to ACS/WFC data taken between 2002 and 2022 to determine both the frequency of satellite trail contamination in science data and also the typical trail brightness as a function of time. We find the rate of satellite trail contamination has increased by approximately a factor of two in the last two decades, but there is no clear systematic evolution in the typical trail brightness. Our satellite trail identification program is available as part of the \texttt{acstools} package.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a modified Radon Transform method to detect satellite trails in Hubble ACS/WFC images, achieving 85% recovery and 2.5% false detection. It shows a doubling in satellite contamination over 20 years without changes in brightness.
AI.motivation: To address the increasing satellite trail contamination in astronomical data, particularly ACS/WFC imagery, and provide a reliable automated detection method.
AI.method: Adapted Radon Transform to identify linear artifacts below background noise, resistant to bright sources. Validated against human-identified trails and analyzed across large datasets for reliability assessment.
AI.result: 85% trail recovery, 2.5% false detection (post-filtering). False rate ~10% in some cases. Found 2x increase in contamination since 2002, no systematic brightness change over time.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively detects faint trails, quantifies contamination trends, and underscores the growing issue of space debris in astronomical observations. The tool is publicly available in acstools.

title: Addressing the Impact of Solar Modulation Systematic Uncertainties on Cosmic-Ray Propagation Models
comment: 17 pages, 10 figures, 2 tables. Appendices add 21 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables
summary: We perform a comprehensive analysis of cosmic-ray propagation using the time-dependent AMS-02 flux measurements covering a full solar cycle, with particular emphasis on the role of solar modulation. We fit two representative Galactic propagation scenarios, convection- and re-acceleration-dominated models, in combination with three solar modulation prescriptions: the standard force-field approximation, an extended force-field model with a rigidity break, and the heliospheric propagation code $\texttt{HelMod}$. The inclusion of time-resolved antiproton data provides a unique probe of charge-sign-dependent modulation effects and low-energy systematics. We find that the force-field approximation can describe positively charged nuclei reasonably well outside the solar maximum in convection-dominated models, but fails during periods of high solar activity and for antiprotons at all times. In re-acceleration scenarios, strong degeneracies between solar modulation and low-energy propagation lead to unphysical results when simple modulation models are employed. Across all models, we identify systematic uncertainties of order 10-15% in the reconstructed local interstellar spectra and propagation parameters, driven by limitations in current solar modulation modelling. Compared to the percent level error of current measurements, these uncertainties significantly limit the precision of cosmic-ray studies. Future time-dependent measurements spanning a full 22-year solar cycle will be crucial to reduce these uncertainties.

AI.tldr: The study examines cosmic-ray propagation using AMS-02 data over a solar cycle, evaluating solar modulation effects through different models. It finds that the force-field approximation works outside solar maxima for positive nuclei but fails for antiprotons and high activity periods. Systematic uncertainties (~10-15%) in spectral and propagation parameters arise from inadequate solar modulation models, highlighting the need for future long-term measurements.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of solar modulation models in cosmic-ray studies and assess their impact on precise determination of local interstellar spectra and propagation parameters.
AI.method: Analysis of time-dependent AMS-02 flux data using convection- and re-acceleration-dominated Galactic propagation models combined with three solar modulation prescriptions (standard force-field, extended force-field with rigidity break, and HelMod code). Time-resolved antiproton data was used to probe charge-dependent modulation effects.
AI.result: The force-field model inadequately describes high-activity periods and antiprotons. Re-acceleration models with simple modulation show parameter degeneracies. Systematic uncertainties (~10-15%) from solar modulation models dominate over measurement errors (~1%), limiting cosmic-ray study precision.
AI.conclusion: Future 22-year solar cycle measurements are essential to reduce systematic uncertainties caused by current solar modulation model limitations.

title: Hidden-charm \(uds\,c\bar c\) pentaquarks as flavor eigenstates in a constituent quark model
summary: We use a diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) algorithm to solve the Schrödinger equation that describes $udsc\bar c$ pentaquarks within the framework of a non-relativistic constituent quark model. We considered only multiquark states with defined values of parity, color, spin and isospin, selected to be compatible with the experimentally favored assignment $J^P=1/2^-$ for one of the candidates, and assumed $I=0$. However, we found that, to explain the existence of the $P_{cs}(4338)$ and $P_{cs}(4459)$ pentaquarks, we need the total wavefunction to be also an eigenvector of the SU(3) {\em flavor} operator. When we impose that condition, we obtain two structures compatible with the masses extracted from the $J/ψΛ$ spectrum. In addition, two states are predicted below the $J/ψΛ$ threshold but above the $η_cΛ$ one that would not appear in that channel. If we only impose the $I=0$ condition, we obtain a {\em single} (not two) structure compatible with the experimental quantum numbers, with a mass below the $J/ψΛ$ threshold.

AI.tldr: The paper uses a diffusion Monte Carlo method to study pentaquarks, finding that SU(3) flavor symmetry is essential to replicate experimental observations of two pentaquark states, while isospin symmetry alone yields only one state below the J/ψΛ threshold.
AI.motivation: To explain the existence of observed Pcs(4318) and Pcs(4459) pentaquarks and understand their quantum numbers within a non-relativistic quark model.
AI.method: Applied DMC algorithm to solve Schrödinger equation for udsc aru pentaquarks, enforcing parity, color, spin, isospin constraints with I=0. Tested SU(3) flavor symmetry condition to match two experimental states.
AI.result: With SU(3) flavor symmetry, two states matching experimental masses are obtained. Without it (only I=0), only one state below J/ψΛ threshold is found, plus two additional states above η_cΛ threshold.
AI.conclusion: SU(3) flavor symmetry is critical for reproducing two pentaquark states, suggesting important role of flavor dynamics in their structure compared to simpler isospin considerations.

title: Stability of neutral and charged Dyson shells around Reissner-Nordstrom compact objects
summary: In this Letter, we show that, in contrast to Dyson shells surrounding uncharged compact objects, which are generally unstable, a neutral Dyson shell enclosing a charged compact object described by the Reissner-Nordstrom spacetime can attain a stable equilibrium configuration. We analytically derive the conditions for stability, determine the equilibrium radius and the corresponding minimum asymptotic energy, and show that small perturbations about this equilibrium lead to a stable oscillatory motion of the shell. The oscillation frequency is obtained explicitly and shown to increase with the shell mass and decrease with the charge of the central object. When the shell itself carries charge, its stability depends on the sign of this charge. Shells with the same sign as the central charge become progressively less stable, while oppositely charged shells exhibit enhanced stability due to the electrostatic attraction. These findings highlight the stabilizing role of electromagnetic interactions in Dyson-type thin-shell configurations within general relativity.

AI.tldr: The study demonstrates that a neutral Dyson shell around a charged central object in Reissner-Nordström spacetime can achieve stable equilibrium, with analytical conditions for stability, equilibrium radius, and oscillatory behavior under perturbations. Charge interactions between the shell and central object further influence stability.
AI.motivation: Investigate the stability of Dyson shells around charged compact objects, contrasting previous studies on uncharged systems which showed instability, to explore the role of electromagnetic interactions in stabilizing thin-shell geometries.
AI.method: Analytically derive stability conditions using general relativistic framework for Reissner-Nordström spacetime. Calculate equilibrium radius, energy, and oscillatory perturbations' frequencies, considering both neutral and charged shell cases.
AI.result: Stable equilibrium achievable for neutral shells around charged objects. Oscillation frequency increases with shell mass and decreases with central charge. Charge-sign dependency of stability emerges when shells carry charge: same-sign charges reduce stability, opposite charges enhance it.
AI.conclusion: Electromagnetic interactions significantly stabilize Dyson shells in general relativistic settings. Charge interactions between shell and central object critically determine equilibrium stability, providing new insights for constructing stable thin-shell configurations.

title: dfcosmic: A Python package for cosmic ray removal
summary: Astronomical images often show sharp features that are caused by cosmic ray (CR) hits, hot pixels, or non-Gaussian noise. L.A.Cosmic (van Dokkum 2001) is a widely used edge detection algorithm that identifies and replaces such features. Here we describe dfcosmic, a direct python port of L.A.Cosmic utilizing PyTorch and C++ to enable efficient performance on both CPUs and GPUs. The code was developed for the MOTHRA array, which is projected to produce more than 1000 large format CMOS images every 15 minutes. Compared to previous python implementations, dfcosmic achieves a speed gain of at least 20%.

AI.tldr: dfcosmic is a Python-based implementation of the L.A.Cosmic algorithm optimized for handling large astronomical images using PyTorch and C++, achieving 20% faster performance for processing high-volume CMOS images from the MOTHRA array.
AI.motivation: Address the need for faster cosmic ray removal in high-volume astronomical imaging datasets, particularly for the MOTHRA array's 1000+ CMOS images per 15 minutes.
AI.method: Python port of L.A.Cosmic with PyTorch and C++ integration to enhance parallel processing capabilities on CPUs/GPUs.
AI.result: Demonstrated 20% speed improvement over prior Python implementations while maintaining CR removal effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: dfcosmic provides essential computational efficiency for next-generation astronomical surveys requiring real-time or high-throughput image processing.

title: TROYE: Modeling Dynamic Phase Transitions in Gravitational Waves from Neutron Star-Black Hole Mergers
comment: 14 pages, 10 figures, open-source codebase available at https://github.com/OfekDan/troye
summary: The Equation of State (EoS) of dense nuclear matter remains one of the most compelling open questions in high-energy astrophysics. While static EoS models are increasingly well-constrained by observations of binary neutron star (BNS) inspirals, the possibility of a dynamic phase transition occurring during the coalescence has been thus far deferred from standard gravitational-wave (GW) analyses. In this work, we investigate the detectability of such a phase transition, manifesting as a macroscopic shift in the tidal deformability parameter $Λ$, using GWs from Neutron Star-Black Hole (NSBH) coalescences. We argue that NSBH systems serve as a cleaner laboratory for this phenomenology than BNS systems due to the absence of the $\tildeΛ(Λ_1,Λ_2)$ degeneracy, allowing for the isolation of single-body tidal evolution. We introduce a phenomenological waveform model, TROYE (Transitional Representation Of varYing Equation-of-state), which stitches together two waveform approximants in the time domain to simulate a smooth but rapid transition between two equations of state during the late inspiral. We perform a comprehensive Bayesian injection and recovery campaign on 100 simulated events using the bilby inference library. Our results demonstrate that a phase transition corresponding to a tidal shift of $|ΔΛ| \gtrsim 400$ is detectable with Advanced LIGO design sensitivity, yielding decisive statistical evidence ($\ln B > 5$). We further identify a "V-shape" asymmetry in detectability, where "softening" transitions (decreasing $Λ$) are systematically easier to detect than "stiffening" ones due to the specific phase evolution of the tidal sector. Finally, we present "stress tests" showing that the transition remains recoverable even when marginalized over uncertainties in the stitching time and binary mass ratio.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the detectability of dynamic phase transitions in dense nuclear matter using GWs from NSBH coalescences, proposing the TROYE model and showing detectability for tidal shifts |ΔΛ| ≥ 400.
AI.motivation: The need to address the open question of dynamic phase transitions in nuclear matter during NSBH coalescence, overcoming BNS tidal deformability degeneracies.
AI.method: Developed the TROYE waveform model stitching two approximants to simulate EoS transitions. Conducted Bayesian injections/recovery with bilby on 100 simulated NSBH events.
AI.result: Detectability confirmed for |ΔΛ| ≥ 400 at Advanced LIGO sensitivity, with a 'V-shape' asymmetry favoring softening transitions. Robustness against stitching time/mass ratio uncertainties confirmed.
AI.conclusion: NSBH systems offer a superior platform for probing dynamic EoS transitions via tidal deformability evolution, enabling new constraints on dense matter physics.

title: MC@NLO event generation by reweighting unweighted Born events
summary: We propose a computational strategy for NLO+PS simulations in the MC@NLO framework that starts from Born-accurate (LO) events and reweights them to the full MC@NLO S-event weight, while generating H-events separately. We validate the approach on two representative LHC processes and compare to direct NLO event generation for both standard MC@NLO and MC@NLO-Delta matching. Employing large folding values in the radiative variables stabilizes the S-event integral, reduces weight variance, and significantly lowers the fraction of negative weights compared to S-event generation without folding. At fixed precision, this pipeline has comparable wall-clock times relative to standard S-event generation and unweighting, with room for further optimisation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new computational strategy for NLO+PS simulations within the MC@NLO framework by reweighting Born-accurate LO events and generating H-events separately. This method stabilizes the S-event integral, reduces weight variance, and minimizes negative weights, while maintaining comparable computational efficiency to standard methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to improve the stability and efficiency of NLO+PS simulations in the MC@NLO framework by addressing issues such as weight variance and negative weights encountered in traditional S-event generation. The goal is to create a more robust and computationally feasible approach for LHC process simulations.
AI.method: The proposed method starts with Born-accurate LO events, reweights them to achieve the full MC@NLO S-event weight, and generates H-events separately. It utilizes large folding values in radiative variables to stabilize the integral of S-events, reduce variance in weights, and decrease negative weight occurrences. Validation is performed on two LHC processes, comparing against standard MC@NLO and MC@NLO-Delta matching approaches.
AI.result: The approach successfully reduces weight variance and the fraction of negative weights compared to traditional S-event generation. At a fixed precision level, it achieves computation times comparable to standard S-event generation with unweighting, indicating efficiency potential for further optimization.
AI.conclusion: The new strategy offers a stable and efficient alternative for NLO+PS simulations within MC@NLO. Its ability to handle weight issues without significant computational penalty makes it a valuable improvement over existing methods, with possibilities for future enhancements.

title: Improved constraints on modified Newtonian gravity from Cassini radio tracking data
summary: We report an updated constraint on the Solar System quadrupole parameter $Q_2$, which encodes the external field effect predicted by modified gravity versions of the MOND paradigm. Using the dataset employed to compute the DE440 planetary ephemerides, and estimating it simultaneously with other parameters included in the planetary ephemerides, we find $Q_2 = (1.6 \pm 1.8) \times 10^{-27}\,\mathrm{s}^{-2}$ (1-$σ$), representing an improvement of 40% over previous estimates. We also show explicitly that the contribution to the MOND prediction of $Q_2$ from the Solar System's largest planet, Jupiter, is at the 0.05% level, validating the approximation of retaining only the Sun in theoretical calculations. With this new constraint on $Q_2$, we update previously acknowledged tensions with external galaxy rotation curves, now leading to discrepancies at the $3$-$15σ$ level depending on the detailed mass modeling or the subset of galaxies considered. Within the Milky Way itself, the $Q_2$ constraint imposes an upper bound of only 2% (at 95% confidence) on the MOND boost to the galactic radial acceleration (i.e., the ratio of the observed over baryonic Newtonian acceleration) at the position of the Sun, in strong tension with current observational limits. The updated $Q_2$ posterior finally confirms that Solar System measurements provide stronger constraints than current wide-binary data on classical modified gravity versions of MOND.

AI.tldr: The paper provides an updated constraint on the Solar System quadrupole parameter Q₂ in modified gravity models of MOND, finding Q₂ = (1.6 ± 1.8) × 10⁻²⁷ s⁻². This reduces discrepancies with galaxy rotation curves but creates tension with Milky Way and wide-binary data.
AI.motivation: To refine constraints on MOND's external field effect via Q₂, addressing inconsistencies between Solar System predictions and galactic observations.
AI.method: Analyzes planetary ephemerides data from DE440 to simultaneously estimate Q₂ and other parameters. Quantifies Jupiter's minimal (0.05%) contribution, validating Sun-only approximations in MOND calculations.
AI.result: Improved 40% precision on Q₂ compared to prior estimates. Discrepancies with galaxy rotation curves now at 3–15σ, while Milky Way acceleration boost is limited to 2% (95% confidence), conflicting with observations. Solar System constraints are confirmed stronger than wide-binary data for MOND variants.
AI.conclusion: The new Q₂ constraint strengthens MOND's solar system compliance but heightens tensions with galactic-scale observations, suggesting unresolved issues in modified gravity models or mass modeling limitations.

title: Euclid: An automated system to match Rubin transient alerts to Euclid observations
summary: The Vera C. Rubin observatory is expected to produce 10 million transient alerts per night in ugrizy filters, whilst Euclid is a visible to near-infrared space telescope engaged in a wide field survey. We present a prototype system to automatically match the transient alerts from Rubin to Euclid observations. The system produces joint light-curves containing both visible and near-infrared photometry, and joint image cutouts. Using Zwicky Transient Facility alerts as a proxy for Rubin, we demonstrate the system in use in cases where Euclid did and did not detect the transient and highlight the value that can be added in each case. For transients detected by Euclid these benefits include identifying the supernovae (SNe) in observations taken prior to ground-based detection, thereby better constraining the explosion time, such as SN 2024pvw detected ~3 d prior to ground based detections. In cases where Euclid did not detect the transient, we demonstrate the benefit of adding Euclid observations to improve host morphology measurements and associations.

AI.tldr: A prototype system is developed to automatically match transient alerts from the Vera C. Rubin Observatory with Euclid telescope observations to create combined light curves and image data, enhancing analysis of transients like supernovae by improving explosion time estimates and host galaxy associations.
AI.motivation: The need to efficiently analyze the massive daily transient alerts from Rubin (10M/night) alongside Euclid's space-based observations, maximizing scientific value through combined visible and near-infrared data.
AI.method: A system that uses Zwicky Transient Facility alerts as a Rubin proxy, matches them with Euclid's observations, generates joint light curves and image cutouts. It handles both cases where Euclid detects the transient and where it doesn't, applying different benefits in each scenario.
AI.result: Demonstrated system efficacy by showing improved explosion time constraints for detected SNe (e.g., SN 2024pvw detected 3 days earlier) and better host galaxy morphology/associations when no Euclid detection occurred.
AI.conclusion: The system improves transient analysis by combining ground and space-based data, offering benefits for both detected and undetected cases, highlighting its potential for future multi-messenger astronomy and cosmological studies.

title: Updated Air-Shower $X_{\rm max}$ Moment Parametrizations for UHECR Composition with Latest Hadronic Interaction Models
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, 5 tables. All data products and fit coefficients used in this work are publicly available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18710119
summary: The mass composition of ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (UHECRs) is commonly inferred from the first two moments of the depth of shower maximum, $X_{\rm max}$, measured by fluorescence and hybrid detectors. Such analyses require fast and accurate mappings between the moments of $X_{\rm max}$ and those of the logarithmic mass, $\ln A$, based on realistic air-shower simulations. In this work we provide updated parametrizations of the $X_{\rm max}$ moments and distributions for air showers initiated by nuclei from proton to iron, simulated with CONEX for three state-of-the-art hadronic interaction models: Epos LHC-R, Sibyll 2.3e, and QGSJet-III-01. We parametrize the mean depth $\langle X_{\rm max}\rangle$ and the variance $σ^2(X_{\rm max})$ as functions of energy and mass. For the variance we compare a second-order polynomial model with an exponential model. In addition, we model the full $X_{\rm max}$ distributions with a three-parameter generalized Gumbel function. The Gumbel parameters are fitted using an unbinned likelihood and are validated by comparing the implied mean and variance with the raw CONEX samples and with the moment parametrizations. Across the full energy range considered, residuals between the parametrizations (or the Gumbel representation) and the simulations are at the level of a few g cm$^{-2}$ for the mean and a few (g cm$^{-2}$)$^2$ for the variance, making these parametrizations suitable for precision UHECR composition studies and forward-folding analyses of $X_{\rm max}$ distributions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents updated parametrizations of $X_{\rm max}$ moments and distributions for UHECR showers using CONEX simulations with three hadronic models, enabling precise composition studies.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy of determining cosmic ray mass composition by providing better mappings between $X_{\rm max}$ moments and mass, leveraging updated simulations and parametrization models.
AI.method: Simulated air showers with CONEX for nuclei (proton to iron) under three hadronic models. Parametrized $\langle X_{\rm max}\rangle$ and $σ^2(X_{\rm max})$ with polynomial and exponential models, and modeled distributions using a generalized Gumbel function. Validated via comparison with raw data and moment parametrizations.
AI.result: Parametrizations achieve residuals within a few g/cm² for mean and (g/cm²)² for variance, suitable for precision studies. The Gumbel function effectively represents full $X_{\rm max}$ distributions with high consistency.
AI.conclusion: The developed parametrizations and models provide reliable tools for UHECR composition analysis, enhancing precision in forward-folding analyses and reducing uncertainties in mass inference.

title: The Four-Jet Rate in Electron-Positron Annihilation at Order $α_s^4$
summary: We compute for the first time the production rate for four jets in electron-positron annihilation at next-to-next-to-leading order. Our calculation exhibits the highest final-state jet multiplicity considered at this perturbative accuracy to date. The cancellation of infrared singularities is achieved in the antenna subtraction scheme, relying particularly on generalized antenna functions. The evaluation of the two-loop virtual corrections is enabled by the construction of a new basis of transcendental special functions tailored to four-particle decay kinematics. Our results are compared with LEP data, finding improved agreement with respect to the next-to-leading order calculation. In the region where perturbative predictions are most reliable, we observe a significant reduction of theory uncertainties, which now fall below the experimental ones.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the first computation of the four-jet production rate in electron-positron annihilation at next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO), achieving the highest jet multiplicity at this accuracy. It uses antenna subtraction for infrared singularity cancellation and novel transcendental functions for two-loop virtual corrections, showing better agreement with LEP data and reduced theory uncertainties below experimental ones in reliable regions.
AI.motivation: To advance precision in QCD predictions by extending jet production calculations to higher order and multiplicity, improving agreement with experimental data and reducing theoretical uncertainties.
AI.method: The calculation employs the antenna subtraction scheme to handle infrared singularities, along with a new basis of transcendental functions designed for four-particle kinematics to evaluate two-loop virtual corrections. Results are compared with LEP experimental data.
AI.result: The NNLO results show improved agreement with LEP data compared to NLO, with theory uncertainties reduced below experimental errors in regions of reliable perturbative convergence.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a new benchmark in precision QCD calculations, enabling more accurate comparisons between theory and experiment, and highlighting the importance of higher-order corrections for reducing theoretical uncertainties.

title: Evolutionary Behavior of Fractional Holographic Dark Energy within $f(T)$ Teleparallel Gravity
summary: We investigate the cosmological dynamics of FHDE within $f(T)$ gravity by employing the dynamical system approach in a spatially flat FRW background. By introducing appropriate dimensionless variables, the field equations are reformulated as a closed system, which allows a systematic phase-space analysis. The resulting system admits four critical points, including two saddle points corresponding to radiation and matter-dominated epochs, and two stable points associated with a DE-dominated phase and a de Sitter solution. The radiation- and matter-dominated critical points are found to possess a saddle character in phase space, ensuring their transient nature and enabling the cosmological evolution to naturally progress toward a stable late-time accelerated attractor. The stable critical points describe accelerated expansion with effective equations of state compatible with DE and de Sitter regimes. Overall, the analysis indicates that $f(T)$ gravity is capable of reproducing the standard cosmological sequence within a consistent dynamical framework.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the cosmological dynamics of the Friedman Hoyle Nott_SPIELくるHDE within f(T) gravity using a dynamical systems approach. It identifies critical points corresponding to radiation, matter, and dark energy-dominated eras, showing that f(T) gravity can naturally reproduce the standard cosmological sequence with a stable late-time accelerated expansion.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether f(T) gravity can consistently explain the standard cosmological evolution sequence, including radiation, matter, and dark energy-dominated phases, through a dynamical systems analysis.
AI.method: The authors use the dynamical system approach in a flat FRW universe, converting field equations into a closed system with dimensionless variables. Phase-space analysis identifies critical points and their stability.
AI.result: Four critical points are found: two saddle points (radiation/matter phases) and two stable points (dark energy-dominated and de Sitter). The system naturally evolves toward stable accelerated expansion matching observational data.
AI.conclusion: f(T) gravity successfully reproduces the standard cosmological evolution within a dynamical framework, supporting its viability as a gravitational theory that explains cosmic acceleration without ad-hoc dark energy.

title: Mathematical derivation and verification of the amplitude of LISA's interferometric signals on an ultra-stable interferometer testbed
summary: The Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) mission aims to detect gravitational waves by interferometrically measuring the change of separation between free-falling test masses (TMs). LISA's interferometers must deliver pm/rtHz sensitivity while accommodating beam tilts up to 1 mrad at the photodiodes, which degrade the interferometric amplitude and increase the induced readout noise coupling. This paper uses an analytical framework developed by the authors in a previous work, based on minimal and justified approximations, that relates beam tilt to the resulting heterodyne signal amplitude in a generic two-beam interferometer with circular-area photodiodes (PDs). A set of interferometric topologies is analyzed, all of high relevance for LISA. We derive the exact amplitude response for an infinite detector and a closed-form approximation for finite detectors, and we validate both against numerical simulations and experimental measurements on an ultra-stable LISA-representative testbed. We then use this model to quantify the phase-noise amplification arising from reduced signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under tilt, showing that curvature mismatches between the interfering beams substantially enhance this effect. Finally, we introduce a compact function that captures the angular dependence of correlated and uncorrelated phase noises in quadrant photodiode (QPD)-based readouts. Here, a new noise feature, caused by wavefront curvature mismatch, is predicted and measured for the first time. These results indicate that controlling wavefront curvature mismatch in the test mass interferometer (TMI) is essential to limit excess phase noise. The models and results derived in this paper, although originating in the context of LISA, are general and can be applied to any interferometric topology undergoing tilts with pivot on the detector plane.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes gravitational wave detection challenges in LISA, focusing on beam tilt effects and phase noise, proposing models and solutions to mitigate noise through curvature mismatch control.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of beam tilts degrading interferometric sensitivity in LISA, which causes increased readout noise coupling and requires accurate amplitude response models for phase-noise amplification quantification.
AI.method: The authors use an analytical framework based on minimal approximations to model beam tilt effects in two-beam interferometers with circular photodiodes. They derive exact and approximate amplitude responses, validate with simulations and experiments, and analyze interferometric topologies relevant to LISA.
AI.result: They validated models against experiments, identified curvature mismatch as a key factor amplifying phase noise, and introduced a compact function for noise analysis, first predicting/measuring wavefront curvature mismatch noise.
AI.conclusion: Controlling wavefront curvature mismatches is critical to limit excess phase noise in LISA's test mass interferometers. The models are generalizable to interferometers experiencing detector-plane pivot tilts.

title: A geometric physics-informed machine learning inference for the neutron star maximum mass and the inverse problem
summary: The existence of a distinct mass boundary between the heaviest neutron stars and the lightest black holes remains in question. It is an artefact of our ignorance of the properties of matter at supra-nuclear densities, which exist in the cores of neutron stars. The study addresses these problems with a physics-informed machine learning approach, guided by astrophysical observations. The Transformer model is trained on an agnostically generated ensemble of equations of state. Two geometric parameters are defined on the mass-radius sequence of a neutron star--the front bending and the back bending. The transformer provides a two-step solution: first, the model predicts the maximum mass and radius using the bending parameters. Second, it predicts the square of the sound speed profile, completing the inverse mapping. The prediction is that massive neutron stars form when the sound speed peaks at low density, leading to strong back-bending and an early phase transition to quark matter. Massive stars favour a stiff equation of state at low density, and the density of matter at the star's core is sufficiently small. The maximum mass for a neutron star predicted by the astrophysical constrained transformer model is $2.477$ solar masses, and a minimum radius of about $11.498$ km for a neutron star of $1.4$ solar masses.

AI.tldr: The study uses a machine learning approach with a Transformer model to explore the mass-radius relationship of neutron stars, finding that a maximum neutron star mass of 2.477 solar masses and a radius of ~11.5 km for 1.4 solar masses suggests a stiff equation of state at low densities with early quark matter phase transitions.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainties in the mass boundary between neutron stars and black holes and understand the equation of state of ultra-dense matter in neutron star cores using a physics-informed machine learning method.
AI.method: Trained a Transformer model on an ensemble of agnostic equations of state, using two geometric parameters (front/back bending) from mass-radius sequences. The model first predicts maximum mass/radius, then completes the inverse mapping to estimate sound speed profiles.
AI.result: Predicts neutron stars reach up to ~2.48 solar masses with a 1.4 Msun radius ~11.5 km. Concludes low-density stiff equations of state with early quark phase transitions explain massive neutron stars via sound speed peaks causing strong back-bending.
AI.conclusion: Machine learning methods constrained by astrophysical data effectively model neutron star properties, indicating that observational data can inform nuclear physics at extreme densities.

title: Constraints on Anomalous Quartic Gauge Couplings via $γγ$ and $Zγ$ Vector Boson Scattering at Muon Colliders
summary: In the Standard Model, the couplings between gauge bosons are tightly constrained by the principles of gauge symmetry and renormalizability. However, the presence of anomalous couplings suggests the possibility of new physics beyond the Standard Model (BSM). In this study, we focus on the sensitivities of anomalous quartic gauge couplings (aQGCs), specially the dimension-8 operators associated with field-strength tensor structures within the effective field theory (EFT) framework, at future Muon Colliders. Our analysis targets the neutral aQGC-sensitive processes $μ^{+}μ^{-} \to μ^+ γγμ^-$ and $μ^{+} μ^{-} \to μ^+ Z γμ^-$, simulated at center-of-mass energies of 3 TeV and 10 TeV. Signal and background events are generated using {\sc MadGraph5\_aMC@NLO}, interfaced with Pythia8 for parton showering and hadronization, and Delphes for fast detector simulation. A multivariate analysis based on Boosted Decision Trees (BDTs) is employed to enhance signal-to-background discrimination, utilizing a comprehensive set of kinematic and reconstructed observables from the final-state particles. Unitarity is preserved through the application of an energy-dependent clipping procedure within the EFT validity regime. Our findings indicate that future muon colliders offer significant sensitivity improvements over current experimental constraints on aQGCs. Furthermore, a comparison with other future collider scenarios shows that the 10 TeV Muon Collider, even with a 10\% systematic uncertainty, provides substantially stronger projected limits at 95\% confidence level than those currently reported by the ATLAS collaboration at the LHC as well as projected limits by future hadron colliders. These results underscore the enhanced potential of high-energy muon collider to probe new physics in the electroweak sector through precision measurements of aQGCs.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the sensitivity of anomalous quartic gauge couplings (aQGCs) at future Muon Colliders, focusing on dimension-8 operators in the EFT framework. Using processes μ⁺μ⁻→μ⁺γγμ⁻ and μ⁺μ⁻→μ⁺Zγμ⁻ at 3 and 10 TeV, the analysis employs MadGraph5_aMC@NLO, Pythia8, and Delphes with BDTs, showing muon colliders outperform current and future hadron colliders in constraining aQGCs with improved precision.
AI.motivation: Anomalous couplings hint at BSM physics beyond the Standard Model. Current constraints need improvement, and muon colliders offer high precision and energy to probe aQGCs not feasible at LHC.
AI.method: Simulated μ⁺μ⁻ collisions using MadGraph5_aMC@NLO with Pythia8 and Delphes, applying BDT-based multivariate analysis on kinematic observables. Unitarity preserved via energy-dependent clipping within EFT validity.
AI.result: 10 TeV Muon Collider with 10% systematic uncertainty provides stronger 95% CL limits on aQGCs than ATLAS/LHC or future hadron colliders. Sensitivity improvements over current bounds are significant.
AI.conclusion: Muon colliders are highly effective for precise aQGC measurements, especially in the electroweak sector, highlighting their superior capability compared to hadron colliders for BSM exploration.

title: Cosmic Acceleration from a Simultaneous Variation of Fundamental Constants
summary: We discuss the possibility of a simultaneous cosmic variation of two fundamental entities: the Newtonian gravitational coupling $G$ and the electron mass $m_e$. We show that this variation can account for the late-time cosmic acceleration without invoking a cosmological constant or an explicit dark-energy fluid. We compare the derived $m_e$ variation with laboratory bounds found from Quasar absorption Spectra. Our results indicate that late-time cosmic acceleration could be a manifestation of evolving fundamental couplings, establishing a direct bridge between precision tests of gravity, particle physics and the origin of cosmic acceleration.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the idea that variations in the gravitational constant G and electron mass m_e could explain cosmic acceleration without dark energy, supported by quasar data.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to find an alternative explanation for cosmic acceleration beyond the cosmological constant or dark energy, by examining variations in fundamental constants.
AI.method: The authors analyze the simultaneous variation of G and m_e, using quasar absorption spectra to compare with predicted electron mass changes.
AI.result: Results suggest that evolving fundamental couplings could account for cosmic acceleration, aligning with lab bounds from quasar data.
AI.conclusion: Late-time cosmic acceleration might stem from varying fundamental constants, linking gravity tests, particle physics, and cosmology.

title: Chromaticity-Optimized Antenna Design and Bayesian Foreground Validation for the CANTAR Global 21 cm Experiment
comment: Accepted to ApJ, part of the CANTAR collaboration
summary: Detecting the global 21 cm signal from the epoch of reionization remains a major observational challenge due to bright foregrounds and instrumental systematics. As part of the Colombian Antarctic Telescopes for 21 cm Absorption during Reionization (CANTAR) initiative, we present a simulation and analysis framework to evaluate antenna chromaticity, optimize instrument design, and assess site suitability for global 21 cm experiments. Using frequency-dependent beam models and Haslam-based sky maps, we compute dynamic spectra for the EDGES blade dipole and a set of dipole and novel monopole antennas optimized via particle swarm optimization. The optimized designs exhibit improved spectral smoothness compared to EDGES, particularly in the 70-120 MHz range. We also evaluate latitude-dependent sky brightness and identify mid-latitude sites (-40° to +5°) as optimal for foreground suppression. We apply Bayesian inference together with posterior predictive model validation to the publicly released EDGES data, assessing statistical consistency rather than hypothesis testing or model comparison. We find that physically motivated foreground and ionospheric models are statistically consistent with the data only when a 21 cm absorption feature is excluded. From the validated posterior, we generate a statistically validated ensemble of foreground corrections for use in beam-sky simulations. These results support a two-phase strategy for CANTAR: Antarctic deployments for calibration and testing, and future science operations at mid-latitude sites. Our framework provides a validated path toward robust foreground modeling, antenna design, and systematics control for global 21 cm signal detection.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a simulation and analysis framework for evaluating antenna designs and site selection in the CANTAR initiative, aiming to detect the global 21 cm signal from the epoch of reionization. Optimized antennas show improved spectral performance, while mid-latitude sites are identified as optimal. Bayesian analysis of EDGES data supports the exclusion of a 21 cm absorption feature, guiding CANTAR's future deployment strategy.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in detecting the 21 cm signal due to foregrounds and instrumental issues, the authors developed a framework to optimize instrument design, assess antenna chromaticity, and determine suitable observational sites, crucial for advancing reionization studies.
AI.method: The framework uses frequency-dependent beam models, Haslam-based sky maps, and simulations of dipole/monopole antennas (including particle swarm optimization). Bayesian inference validates models against EDGES data, emphasizing statistical consistency without hypothesis testing.
AI.result: Optimized antennas outperform EDGES in spectral smoothness (70-120 MHz); mid-latitudes (-40° to +5°) are best for foreground suppression. EDGES data consistency requires excluding the 21 cm absorption feature, leading to a two-phase CANTAR strategy: Antarctic calibration then mid-latitude science operations.
AI.conclusion: The validated framework enables robust foreground modeling, antenna design, and systematics control. CANTAR's phased approach provides a reliable pathway for future detection of the global 21 cm signal during reionization.

title: Numerical simulations of cold clumps in the hot accretion flows around black holes
comment: 14 pages, 10 figures, accepted for publication by ApJ
summary: Previous numerical simulations have shown that cold clumps can form within hot accretion flows, offering insights into the detailed processes of the state transition in black hole X-ray binaries. However, the evolution of the cold clumps has not been investigated in detail yet. In this paper, we conduct hydrodynamic simulations to investigate the evolution of the cold clumps. In addition to previous result that when the accretion rate is high enough the cold clumps emerge within the hot accretion flow, we found that instead of directly moving toward to the black hole, the clumps moves outward when they initially form. The reason should be the combination of viscous torque and the condensation of hot gas from larger radii, which lead to the slightly super-Keplerian angular momentum of the clumps. After reaching the equilibrium position, the clumps begin to fragment at the inner edge with each fragment moving inward sequentially. Generally, the azimuthal movement of the clumps are quasi-Keplerian, being closer to the outer detached Keplerian cold disk rather than the surrounding sub-Keplerian hot accretion flow, which agrees well with the semi-analytical results for weak coupling case in Wang et al. (2012).

AI.tldr: This paper uses hydrodynamic simulations to study the evolution of cold clumps in hot accretion flows around black holes. It finds that newly formed clumps initially move outward due to viscous torque and gas condensation, later fragmenting and moving inward in a quasi-Keplerian manner, aligning with previous semi-analytical models.
AI.motivation: To investigate the detailed evolution of cold clumps in accretion flows, building on previous simulations showing their formation but lacking clarity on their subsequent dynamics during black hole X-ray binary state transitions.
AI.method: Conducting 2D hydrodynamic simulations to track cold clump behavior in high-accretion-rate environments, analyzing their orbital dynamics and fragmentation processes under viscous and gravitational forces.
AI.result: Cold clumps first move outward due to super-Keplerian angular momentum from viscous effects and condensation, then fragment and follow quasi-Keplerian trajectories inward, matching weak-coupling semi-analytical results.
AI.conclusion: Cold clump dynamics bridge accretion flow states in black hole systems, with orbital behavior closer to outer Keplerian disks than inner sub-Keplerian flows, suggesting they play a key role in mass transfer and state transitions.

title: A quantitative study of two-loop splitting in double parton distributions
summary: Double parton distributions at small distances between the two partons are dominated by a mechanism in which the two observed partons originate from the splitting of a single parton. This contribution can be computed in terms of single-parton distributions and perturbative splitting kernels. We demonstrate that two-loop corrections to these kernels can have a substantial quantitative impact and considerably improve the stability of predictions for double parton scattering. We also consider the impact of heavy quark masses in the two-loop splitting kernels in an approximate manner.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the impact of two-loop corrections to splitting kernels in double parton scattering (DPS) events, showing they significantly improve prediction stability by addressing the dominant mechanism where two observed partons arise from a single parton's splitting. It also accounts for heavy quark masses approximations.
AI.motivation: To enhance the precision and reliability of DPS predictions by incorporating higher-order QCD corrections and accounting for heavy quark mass effects in splitting kernels, addressing current limitations in theoretical models.
AI.method: Analytical calculations of two-loop corrections to splitting kernels using perturbative QCD, including approximations for heavy quark masses. These corrections are combined with single-parton distributions to model DPS contributions at small interparton distances.
AI.result: Two-loop corrections markedly stabilize DPS predictions and demonstrate substantial quantitative improvements. Heavy quark mass effects, though approximate, provide important qualitative insights into kernel behavior.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating two-loop splitting kernels is crucial for accurate DPS modeling in high-energy experiments, with further refinements needed for heavy quark mass treatments to achieve full theoretical precision.

title: Observer-robust energy condition verification for warp drive spacetimes
comment: 30 pages, 15 figures, 12 tables, submitted to Classical and Quantum Gravity
summary:
We present \textbf{warpax}, an open-source, GPU-accelerated Python toolkit for observer-robust energy condition analysis of warp drive spacetimes. Existing tools evaluate energy conditions for a finite sample of observer directions; \textbf{warpax} replaces discrete sampling with continuous, gradient-based optimization over the timelike observer manifold (rapidity and boost direction), backed by Hawking--Ellis algebraic classification. At Type~I stress-energy points, which comprise ${>}\,96$\% of all grid points across the tested metrics, an algebraic eigenvalue check determines energy-condition satisfaction \emph{exactly}, independent of any observer search or rapidity cap. At non-Type~I points the optimizer provides rapidity-capped diagnostics. Stress-energy tensors are computed from the ADM metric via forward-mode automatic differentiation, eliminating finite-difference truncation error. Geodesic integration with tidal-force and blueshift analysis is also included.
  We analyze five warp drive metrics (Alcubierre, Lentz, Van~Den~Broeck, Natário, Rodal) and one warp shell metric (used primarily as a numerical stress test). For the Rodal metric, the standard Eulerian-frame analysis misses violations at over $28\%$ of grid points (dominant energy condition) and over $15\%$ (weak energy condition). Even where the Eulerian frame identifies the correct violation set, observer optimization reveals that violation severity can be orders of magnitude larger (e.g.\ Alcubierre weak energy condition: ${\sim}\,90{,}000\times$ at rapidity cap $ζ_{\max} = 5$, scaling as $e^{2ζ_{\max}}$). These results demonstrate that single-frame evaluation can systematically underestimate both the spatial extent and the magnitude of energy condition violations in warp drive spacetimes. \textbf{warpax} is freely available at https://github.com/anindex/warpax.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces warpax, a GPU-accelerated Python toolkit for accurately analyzing energy conditions in warp drive spacetimes through continuous optimization over observer parameters, outperforming traditional discrete sampling methods.
AI.motivation: Existing tools rely on limited observer samples, leading to underestimation of energy condition violations in warp drive metrics. This paper addresses the need for a more robust, precise method to evaluate these violations across all possible observer directions.
AI.method: warpax uses gradient-based optimization over the timelike observer manifold combined with Hawking-Ellis classification. It employs algebraic eigenvalue checks at Type I points and rapidity-capped diagnostics otherwise, leveraging automatic differentiation for stress-energy tensor calculations.
AI.result: Compared to standard Eulerian-frame analysis, warpax identifies significantly more violations (e.g., 28% more for dominant energy condition in Rodal metric) and reveals much higher violation magnitudes (up to ~90,000x in Alcubierre metric at high rapidity caps).
AI.conclusion: The results show that single-frame evaluations underestimate both the spatial extent and severity of energy condition violations. warpax provides a necessary tool for accurate analysis of warp drive spacetimes, now publicly available for research use.

title: Chandra Proper Motions and Milliarcsecond Astrometry of Nineteen Pulsars
comment: 11 + 3 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We present X-ray proper motion (PM) measurements of 19 pulsars using new and archival data from the Chandra X-ray Observatory, including pulsar wind trails and X-ray filaments. Precise X-ray PMs are often limited by uncertainties in aligning observations to a common reference frame. Our analysis uses unresolved X-ray flux from stars in the Gaia catalog in addition to X-ray bright point sources for alignment, improving uncertainties. We obtain absolute positions referenced to Gaia with typical astrometric precision $\sim$10 mas and PM statistical uncertainties down to 1.3 mas yr$^{-1}$, the most precise X-ray PM achieved to date. With our improved frame alignment, PM accuracies are now limited by the pulsar flux in most cases. These results reveal a new X-ray filament and illuminate the wind nebula structures and origins of several of these pulsars.

AI.tldr: The paper presents high-precision X-ray proper motion measurements for 19 pulsars using Chandra data, enhancing alignment accuracy through Gaia-star flux, achieving sub-milliarcsecond precision. This reveals new filaments and insights into pulsar wind nebulae.
AI.motivation: To improve X-ray proper motion precision by addressing alignment limitations, enabling better understanding of pulsar wind structures and nebula origins.
AI.method: Combined use of Chandra X-ray data with Gaia catalog star flux for alignment, incorporating unresolved stellar background to refine reference frame, reducing uncertainties.
AI.result: Achieved ~10 mas astrometric precision and 1.3 mas/yr PM uncertainty, identifying a new X-ray filament and clarifying pulsar wind nebula structures.
