AI.conclusion: Enhanced X-ray PM techniques now limit further progress to pulsar flux constraints, with implications for studying pulsar dynamics and nebula formation.

title: Quark-meson diquark model and color superconductivity in dense quark matter
summary: We consider the two- and three-flavor QMD models as renormalizable low-energy models for QCD at finite quark chemical potentials with quarks, mesons, and diquarks as effective degrees of freedom. Using the on-shell scheme the parameters in the scalar sector can be fixed and expressed in terms of observed meson masses and decay constants. The remaining parameters can be varied. In the QMD models, all the symmetries are global, including the $SU(N_c)$ symmetry. The breaking of the global symmetries gives rise to a number of Goldstone bosons depending on the symmetry-breaking pattern, i.e. whether the system is in the 2SC phase or the color-flavor-locked (CFL) phase. This is in contrast to perturbative QCD, where some of the gauge bosons become massive via the Higgs mechanism. We classify the Goldstone bosons and show that their type and number are in accordance with general counting rules. The thermodynamic potential $Ω$ is calculated in the mean-field approximation, where we include quark loops, while mesons and diquarks are treated at tree level. As important applications, we study the properties of the pion-condensed phase at finite isospin chemical potential, and the 2SC and CFL phases at finite baryon chemical potential. We present a few numerical results focusing on the speed of sound, gaps, and condensates. It is shown that the BCS gaps approaches a constant for large isospin and baryon chemical potentials and that the speed of sound approaches the conformal value from above in the same limit.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes two-flavor and three-flavor QMD models as renormalizable low-energy QCD models at finite chemical potentials, focusing on symmetry breaking patterns, Goldstone bosons classification, and phase diagrams with applications to pion-condensed, 2SC, and CFL phases. Numerical results on gaps and speed of sound are presented.
AI.motivation: To study QCD at finite baryon/isospin chemical potentials using effective models that capture symmetry breaking, Goldstone boson counts, and phase transitions, unlike perturbative QCD which doesn't adequately describe these phenomena.
AI.method: Used QMD models with quarks, mesons, diquarks as degrees of freedom; fixed scalar sector parameters via on-shell scheme with observed meson masses/decay constants. Calculated thermodynamic potential in mean-field approximation with quark loops, while mesons/diquarks treated at tree level. Analyzed symmetry breaking patterns and classified Goldstone bosons for 2SC/CFL phases.
AI.result: Confirmed Goldstone boson counts match general symmetry-breaking rules. Demonstrated BCS gaps reach constant values at high chemical potentials. Shown speed of sound approaches conformal value from above in high mu limits. Characterized phase properties (condensates) in pion-condensed, 2SC, and CFL phases.
AI.conclusion: QMD models effectively capture nonperturbative QCD behavior at finite chemical potentials, correctly reproducing Goldstone modes and phase transitions. Results support use of these models for studying quark matter and dense QCD phenomena.

title: Decay of a multi-axionic SU(N) symmetric color aether in the early Universe as an origin of emergence of a many-component dark matter
summary: We establish a new SU(N) symmetric model of interaction between the fields of four types: the multiplet of vector fields, which describes the so-called color aether, the multiplet of pseudoscalar fields, which is associated with the multi-component cosmic dark matter, the gauge and gravitational fields. The extended Lagrangian of the model contains a new constructive element, which is based on the covariant SU(N) symmetric divergence of the multiplet of vector fields; this new element, being the multiplet of scalars from the point of view of spacetime transformations and the color vector from the point of view of the SU(N) group space, gives us the possibility to formulate properly the multi-axionic extension of the Peccei-Quinn theory. The hypothesis of a spontaneous polarization of the multi-axionic color aether in the early Universe is presented. The set of self-consistent master equations of the model is derived. An application to cosmology is considered: the obtained master equations are solved for the truncated test model based on the Bianchi-I spacetime platform.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an SU(N) symmetric model incorporating vector fields (color aether), pseudoscalar fields (multi-component dark matter), and gauge/gravity fields. A new Lagrangian term based on the SU(N) divergence of vector fields enables a multi-axionic extension of the Peccei-Quinn theory. The model explores spontaneous polarization of the color aether in early Universe and solves equations using Bianchi-I cosmology.
AI.motivation: To address theoretical gaps in unifying dark matter and axion physics while maintaining SU(N) symmetry, extending Peccei-Quinn theory to multi-axionic scenarios and exploring early universe dynamics through a novel field interaction framework.
AI.method: Constructed a covariant SU(N) symmetric Lagrangian with a divergence term of vector fields as both spacetime scalars and SU(N) vectors. Derived self-consistent master equations via variational principles, applied to Bianchi-I cosmological spacetime for analytical solutions.
AI.result: Successfully formulated a new model combining multi-axion dark matter and color aether interactions. Derived solutions for a truncated Bianchi-I model showing consistency with cosmological principles.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a viable framework for studying multi-component axion physics and early universe dynamics, offering new avenues for testing SU(N) symmetric cosmological scenarios through astrophysical observations.

title: GR-Athena++: Binary Neutron Star Merger Simulations with Neutrino Transport
summary: We present general-relativistic radiation magnetohydrodynamics simulations of binary neutron star mergers performed with GR-Athena++. Neutrino transport is treated using a moment-based, energy-integrated scheme (M1), augmented by neutrino number density evolution (N0). Our implementation is validated through an extensive suite of standard tests and demonstrated to perform robustly under adaptive mesh refinement. As a first application, we simulate the gravitational collapse of a uniformly rotating, magnetized neutron star, demonstrating stable radiation evolution through apparent-horizon formation using a novel excision technique based on the tapering of state vector evolution inside the horizon. To further test robustness in highly dynamic environments, we apply our code to two demanding binary neutron star merger scenarios. We investigate a long-lived remnant with the DD2 equation of state, evolved with full general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamics and M1 neutrino transport. Following this, a gravitational collapse scenario with the SFHo equation of state is explored. We showcase long-term stable evolution on neutrino cooling time-scales, demonstrating robust handling of excision and stable evolution of the post-collapse accretion phase in three-dimensional mergers with magnetic fields and neutrino radiation.

AI.tldr: The paper describes advanced simulations of binary neutron star mergers using GR-Athena++ with M1 neutrino transport and N0 schemes, demonstrating robust handling of gravitational collapse and accretion phases in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy and robustness of simulations in modeling binary neutron star mergers by incorporating general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamics, neutrino transport, and adaptive mesh refinement techniques.
AI.method: Simulations were performed using GR-Athena++ with M1 neutrino transport and N0 schemes. Validated through standard tests, adaptive mesh refinement, and novel excision techniques. Tested on neutron star collapse and two BNS merger scenarios (DD2 and SFHo equations of state).
AI.result: Successful long-term stable evolution of post-collapse accretion phases, robust handling of excision, and demonstration of neutrino cooling time-scale simulations. Validated code performance in dynamic mergers.
AI.conclusion: The implementation enables accurate modeling of neutron star mergers, providing insights into remnant evolution and gravitational wave sources, paving the way for detailed studies of these extreme astrophysical events.

title: On the simulated kinematic distributions of semileptonic $B$ decays
summary: Modern measurements in flavour physics rely on accurate simulations of signal and background processes, provided by a wide range of general-purpose and specialised Monte-Carlo event generators. Due to the inclusion of a larger amount of specialised decays of heavy hadrons, EvtGen is often the tool of choice for many scenarios. We investigate the phase-space sampling algorithm of EvtGen and demonstrate that it generates unphysical features in kinematic distributions of semileptonic $B$ decays involving resonances, originating from neglected phase-space factors. We provide a short-term solution to correct the affected simulated samples through reweighting of the hadronic invariant mass distribution.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a flaw in EvtGen's phase-space sampling algorithm leading to unphysical features in semileptonic B decay distributions due to neglected phase-space factors and proposes a reweighting solution for affected samples.
AI.motivation: Accurate flavour physics simulations require fixing EvtGen's algorithm to avoid biases in kinematic distributions involving resonances.
AI.method: Analyzing EvtGen's phase-space sampling, identifying missing factors causing biases, and applying hadronic invariant mass reweighting as a correction.
AI.result: Demonstrated correction of unphysical features through reweighting, validating improved distribution accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Short-term reweighting effectively mitigates identified biases, emphasizing the need for long-term EvtGen algorithmic improvements.

title: On type II(D) Einstein spacetimes in six dimensions
comment: 6 pages; short summary of 2505.10532 [gr-qc]. Proceedings of "60 Years in Mathematical Physics: Honoring Prof. Metin Gürses", Ankara, December 26th, 2025 (https://sites.google.com/view/gurses2025)
summary: After a concise overview of Einstein spacetimes of type II (or more special) in four and five dimensions, we summarize recent results in the six-dimensional case. We assume the optical matrix to be non-degenerate and ``generic'', and the Weyl tensor to fall off sufficiently rapidly at infinity. As it turns out, the most general metric is characterized by one discrete (normalized) and three continuous parameters, is of type D and belongs to the Kerr-Schild class. Its relation to the previously known Kerr-(A)dS and Kerr-NUT-(A)dS metrics is clarified.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a classification of six-dimensional Einstein spacetimes under specific conditions, showing they are characterized by four parameters and belong to the Kerr-Schild class, extending known lower-dimensional metrics.
AI.motivation: To extend the understanding of higher-dimensional spacetimes beyond the well-studied four and five-dimensional cases, particularly focusing on type D Einstein metrics and their relation to known Kerr-(A)dS and Kerr-NUT-(A)dS solutions.
AI.method: Analysis of six-dimensional Einstein spacetimes with non-degenerate, generic optical matrices and rapidly decaying Weyl tensors. Utilizes properties of Kerr-Schild metrics and algebraic classification of the Weyl tensor.
AI.result: Derivation of a general metric form in six dimensions requiring one discrete and three continuous parameters, confirmed as type D and part of the Kerr-Schild class, thereby unifying and generalizing previous lower-dimensional results.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a framework for six-dimensional Einstein spacetimes' structure, providing a foundation for further study in higher-dimensional general relativity and black hole physics.

title: Detection prospects of solar $g$-modes with LISA
summary: The possibility of detecting solar oscillation modes using space-based gravitational-wave detectors has been investigated in the context of gravitational-wave interferometry, with Polnarev \cite{Polnarev:2009xf} demonstrating that low-frequency solar modes could, in principle, produce detectable signals in a LISA-type interferometer. Motivated by this work, I revisit the problem using current solar models, updated detector sensitivities, and improved theoretical and observational constraints on mode amplitudes. In this study, I compute the gravitational response of solar oscillation modes using standard solar models generated with \texttt{MESA}, and mode eigenfrequencies and eigenfunctions calculated with \texttt{GYRE}. I focus primarily on solar $g$ modes, evaluating their responses for degree $l=2$ and azimuthal orders $m=0$ and $m=2$. The analysis incorporates both the earlier proposed and the current updated LISA sensitivity curves, and I perform a comparative assessment with the TianQin mission in the relevant low-frequency band. To assess the robustness of the predicted signals, I estimate the gravitational responses using two different standard solar models based on the GS98 and AGSS09 abundance compilations. I find that the resulting signal responses are nearly identical for the two models, indicating that uncertainties in solar metallicity have a negligible impact on the detectability of solar $g$ modes by space-based interferometers.

AI.tldr: Investigates the detection of solar g-modes using space-based gravitational-wave detectors like LISA and TianQin, using updated solar models and sensitivity curves.
AI.motivation: Building on Polnarev's previous work, the study revisits solar mode detection with current models, detector sensitivities, and constraints to improve feasibility assessments.
AI.method: Uses MESA for standard solar models, GYRE for mode calculations, evaluates gravitational responses for l=2 g-modes with m=0 and 2, compares LISA and TianQin sensitivity curves, and tests GS98/AGSS09 abundance models.
AI.result: Signal responses show no significant difference between GS98 and AGSS09 models, suggesting metallicity uncertainties don't affect g-mode detectability.
AI.conclusion: Modern space-based detectors could detect solar g-modes with current technology, as solar composition variations have minimal impact on signal detectability.

title: Two-over-Two Lattice Flavor from a Single Flavon with Three Messenger Chains
summary: Flavor hierarchies are organized by a single parameter $B\simeq 5.357$ in a single-flavon Froggatt--Nielsen (FN) framework, in which each effective Yukawa entry arises from the sum of \emph{three} unit-magnitude messenger chains. We present benchmark complex $O(1)$ Yukawa matrices that reproduce quark and charged-lepton masses at $M_Z$ as powers of $ε\equiv 1/B$. The organizing principle is a two-over-two (2/2) lattice of quadrilateral mass ratios, which maps directly to a rational lattice of FN exponents. Sequential dominance preserves the leading-power exponent matrices, while subleading messenger chains generate entry-dependent complex $O(1)$ coefficients and provide a UV-friendly origin for CP violation. Neutrino masses are discussed at the level of eigenvalues within the same $B^n$ counting.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified approach to explain flavor hierarchies in quarks and charged leptons using a single-flavon Froggatt-Nielsen (FN) model with a parameter B≈5.357. The model uses three unit-magnitude messenger chains per Yukawa entry, organized by a 2/2 lattice of mass ratios, and it accounts for CP violation through subleading terms. Neutrino masses are also considered within this framework.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a simple and unified explanation for the flavor hierarchy problem in the Standard Model, particularly addressing quark and charged lepton mass ratios, and the origin of CP violation. Existing models often struggle with complexity or unnatural assumptions, so this framework aims for a more systematic and UV-complete approach.
AI.method: The model employs a single-flavon FN mechanism with a parameter B to generate hierarchical Yukawa matrices. Each Yukawa entry is the sum of three messenger chains (amplitudes) of unit magnitude. A two-over-two lattice of quadrilateral mass ratios maps to FN exponents, preserving leading powers under sequential dominance while allowing subleading terms to generate CP-violating phases and complex coefficients.
AI.result: Benchmark complex O(1) Yukawa matrices are constructed that reproduce observed quark/lepton masses at M_Z scale as powers of ε=1/B. The framework naturally incorporates CP violation through subleading terms, and neutrino masses are analyzed within the same hierarchical counting scheme.
AI.conclusion: The model successfully organizes flavor hierarchies with minimal parameters and provides a UV-friendly mechanism for CP violation. It unifies quark, lepton, and neutrino sectors under a common B^n power-counting structure, offering a testable framework for future collider and neutrino experiments.

title: The Emergence of Measured Geometry in Self-Gravitating Systems
summary: This work investigates the geometrical properties of self-gravitating $N$-body systems from the perspective established by Henri Poincaré and Albert Einstein concerning the operational nature of measured geometry. Utilizing recent numerical analyses of central configurations--special equilibrium solutions to the Newtonian $N$-body problem--we uncover systematic spatial variations in nearest-neighbor particle separations correlated with the radial distance from the system's center of mass. We argue that these variations reflect a context-dependent, emergent effective geometry shaped by gravitational interactions, in accordance with Poincaré's assertion that measured geometry depends on the forces influencing measuring devices, and Einstein's view that rods and clocks define physical geometry through their local dynamics. By revisiting these foundational insights within a modern computational framework, we provide evidence that geometry in self-gravitating Newtonian systems is not a fixed background, but an emergent construct arising from internal physical interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that in self-gravitating N-body systems, geometry isn't fixed but emerges from gravitational interactions, as evidenced by spatial variations in particle separations linked to system dynamics, aligning with Poincaré and Einstein's views on operational geometry.
AI.motivation: To explore how gravitational interactions shape measured geometry in N-body systems, addressing foundational questions about the nature of geometry as operationalized through physical interactions, as proposed by Poincaré and Einstein.
AI.method: Analyzing numerical data on central configurations (equilibrium solutions of the N-body problem) to identify correlations between particle separations and radial distance from the center of mass, then interpreting these variations as indicators of emergent effective geometry.
AI.result: Systematic spatial variations in nearest-neighbor separations were found with radial distance, suggesting geometry emerges context-dependently from system's internal gravitational dynamics rather than existing as a fixed background.
AI.conclusion: Geometry in Newtonian self-gravitating systems is an emergent property arising from physical interactions, supporting Poincaré's and Einstein's operational views by demonstrating geometry's dependence on local gravitational conditions.

title: Cartography of LNV dim-9 SMEFT: Implications for Radiative Neutrino Masses and $0νββ$
summary: We perform a systematic study of lepton-number-violating (LNV) dimension-9 operators in the Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) that can mediate neutrinoless double beta decay ($0νββ$) at tree level, and map them to their possible tree-level ultraviolet completions. Using a diagram-based classification, we enumerate all such completions and isolate minimal two-particle models that avoid generating the dimension-5 Weinberg operator or dimension-7 LNV operators at tree level. We then chart how these minimal models populate the operator landscape and organise them by the loop order at which they radiatively induce lower-dimensional LNV operators, highlighting scenarios in which the tree-level dimension-9 contribution can compete with or dominate loop-suppressed neutrino-mass (dimension-5) effects. Representative one-loop and two-loop classes are matched onto the SMEFT, and their implications for neutrino masses, charged-lepton flavour violation, and the relative size of dimension-9 versus dimension-5 contributions to $0νββ$ are analysed, delineating regions of parameter space where upcoming experiments can be sensitive to genuinely short-range LNV dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper explores lepton-number-violating (LNV) dimension-9 operators in the Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) that can mediate neutrinoless double beta decay ($0νββ$) at tree level. It identifies minimal models avoiding lower-dimensional LNV operators, maps their contributions, and evaluates their detectability in future experiments.
AI.motivation: To systematically study dimension-9 LNV operators in SMEFT that mediate $0νββ$ decay without inducing lower-dimensional operators (e.g., dimension-5 Weinberg or dimension-7 LNV terms), thereby isolating scenarios where tree-level dimension-9 effects could overpower radiative dimension-5 contributions.
AI.method: The authors enumerated all possible tree-level ultraviolet completions for dimension-9 operators using a diagram-based classification. They constructed minimal two-particle models, analyzed their radiative impacts on lower-dimensional operators, and performed one/two-loop SMEFT matching to assess implications for neutrino masses, charged-lepton flavour violation, and $0νββ$ decay rates.
AI.result: The study highlights minimal models whose tree-level dimension-9 contributions can compete with or dominate over dimension-5 neutrino mass effects in $0νββ$. It identifies parameter regions where upcoming experiments could probe short-range LNV dynamics, distinguishing contributions from higher-dimensional operators.
AI.conclusion: The analysis underscores the potential observability of genuine short-range LNV dynamics via $0νββ$ decay and other observables, particularly in scenarios where dimension-9 operators outperform radiative dimension-5 effects. This paves the way for future experimental tests of SMEFT contributions from minimal UV models.

title: Dynamical wormholes
comment: 47 pages, 21 figures. Includes partial review of arXiv:2507.00109
summary: We numerically investigate the dynamical evolution of spherically symmetric charge free wormholes. We concentrate on two specific examples, both of which exhibit wormhole expansion and wormhole collapse: the Ellis-Bronnikov wormhole, which is sourced by a real massless ghost scalar field, and the quantum corrected Schwarzschild black hole in semiclassical gravity (which has a wormhole structure and is not a true black hole), which is sourced by a renormalized energy-momentum tensor. Despite their very different sources, we demonstrate that the dynamics of these two wormholes are remarkably similar. Our analysis focuses on diagrams for the areal radius and components of the energy-momentum tensor. This work also serves as a review, offering a detailed description of how to perform a spherically symmetric dynamical evolution using double null coordinates as well as a review of the static solutions for our two examples.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the dynamical evolution of two spherically symmetric, charge-free wormholes with different sources, showing their similar dynamics despite different origins. It provides methods for simulating such systems using double null coordinates.
AI.motivation: To understand how wormholes evolve dynamically and compare behaviors between distinct source types (ghost scalar field vs quantum-corrected black holes), while offering a methodological guide for future simulations.
AI.method: Numerical simulations using double null coordinates to analyze the areal radius and energy-momentum tensor components for Ellis-Bronnikov and quantum-corrected Schwarzschild wormholes.
AI.result: Both wormhole types exhibit similar expansion and collapse dynamics despite differing sources. The study validates the simulation method's effectiveness for studying dynamical wormhole行为.
AI.conclusion: Dynamical behavior of wormholes can be similar even with different energy sources, suggesting universal features in their evolution. The methodology provides a robust framework for further studies in semiclassical gravity and wormhole physics.
